comments.post_body:
R.I.P. The "Easy Apply" button. 🪦

Claude 4.5 Opus just made it obsolete.

I’ve stress-tested pretty much every AI model for writing cold outreach: GPT-4, Grok, Llama, the whole lineup.

They all fail the same way.

You can smell the AI from the first line.

“Hope you’re doing well…” 🤢

Then I tried Claude 4.5 and it was the first time an LLM didn’t sound like it was pretending to be human.

It actually understands professional dynamics.

Not just what to say, but why you’d say it.

I work closely with hiring teams, so I know exactly which messages get ignored and which ones spark real conversations. I spent ~5 hours dialing in a prompt that makes Claude 4.5 write networking DMs that consistently get responses.

What changed?
✔️ Messages that read like they came from a peer
 ✔️ Hooks grounded in real company context (no hallucinations)
 ✔️ Questions recruiters want to answer
 ✔️ Zero “please hire me” energy

The system does three things extremely well:
 → Breaks down the JD + your actual experience
 → Maps your skills to the team’s unspoken pain points
 → Crafts a “warm intro” that feels unmistakably human

I’m sharing the exact Claude 4.5 networking prompt plus a short guide on how to use it.

If you’re still relying on generic cover letters or hoping Easy Apply works someday… this is a much faster path to the top of the pile.

Comment “OPUS” below (and make sure we’re connected so I can DM you) and I’ll send it over for free.
comments.context_summary: The author is discussing how Claude 4.5 Opus has made the 'Easy Apply' button obsolete by generating highly effective cold outreach messages that sound human and spark real conversations, and is offering to share the exact prompt and guide for free. The commenter is responding to the post by expressing interest in learning more about this new approach to networking and job applications.

comments.post_body:
I’m happy to share that I’m starting a new position as Senior Vice President at JPMorganChase!

VP → SVP It’s not just a prefix.
It’s a perspective shift.

From VP — where I learned to build, scale, and lead with intent
To SVP, where the stakes are higher, the waters deeper, and the current stronger.

This new chapter is about:
- Architecting not just systems, but succession
- Scaling not just throughput, but trust
- Leading not just with logic, but with legacy


To the mentors who sharpened my instincts, Sudhan Arputharaj Sivasonai VellayaPillai Tarun Goyal Srinivasan Krishnamoorthy Shouri Varanasi Haja Mohideen Asif Nawaz Shah Nawaz, PMP Sri Balaji Ramakrishnan Shashank Tandon & more!

To the teams who swam beside me through every migration, Sai Bhaskar Reddy Pothamsetti Reshma Chinta Raj Kumar Herman Arulappan & more!

And to the tides that tested my tailfin—Thank you
comments.context_summary: The commenter is congratulating the author on their new position as Senior Vice President at JPMorganChase and reflecting on the lessons learned from their previous role as VP. The author is expressing gratitude to mentors and teams who have supported them throughout their career and looking forward to the new challenges and responsibilities that come with their new role.

comments.post_body:
r.i.p. applying to 100 jobs a week.

AI just made it obsolete.

i've tested every job search strategy. mass applications, networking, recruiter outreach, resume optimization...

none of it led to reliable interviews.

then i built an AI that fixes job search from the ground up.

it's called poozle, and it uses machine learning to pre-qualify you for roles before you waste time applying.

here's how it works:

most job boards show you everything and make you guess which ones are worth your time.

poozle's AI does the opposite: 
→ it analyzes your background (experience, skills, career trajectory) 
→ it scans 100,000+ job postings in real-time 
→ it calculates your statistical match score for each role 
→ it only shows you jobs where you're likely to land an interview

then it does something no other platform does: it caps applicant pools at 50.

when the AI shows you a role, you're guaranteed to be one of the first 50 applicants. not applicant 487.

the result: you apply to 10 jobs instead of 100. and you get 4x more interviews.

here's what makes the AI different:
→ pre-qualification matching: it doesn't just keyword match. it understands your actual experience and maps it to company needs 
→ real-time applicant tracking: it knows when a role hits 50 applicants and stops showing it 
→ freshness ranking: it prioritizes jobs that are actively scheduling interviews 
→ swipe interface: find a match? swipe right. bad fit? swipe left. the AI learns from every decision.

job seekers are using this to escape the "easy apply" black hole.

here's what the difference looks like:

without the AI (standard job boards): 
→ scroll through 1,000s of jobs manually 
→ guess which ones are worth applying to 
→ spend 20 min per application 
→ compete with 800+ people 
→ get ghosted 95% of the time

with the AI (poozle): 
→ open the app 
→ AI shows your top pre-qualified matches 
→ swipe right on 7 of them (takes 30 seconds) 
→ you're applicant number 12, 8, and 23 
→ interview requests within a week

same resume. same skills. different AI.

the job search isn't a human problem anymore. it's an AI problem.

and most people are still using 2015 tools.

i'm giving away free access to the AI.

if you're tired of applying to black holes, this is your shortcut.

comment "POOZLE" below (make sure we're connected so i can DM you) and i'll send you the download link + free pro access.

available on ios + android.

p.s. the AI works best when you let it learn your preferences. swipe on 20-30 jobs and it gets scary good at showing you perfect matches.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about Poozle, an AI-powered job search platform that pre-qualifies candidates for roles and limits applicant pools to 50, aiming to increase the efficiency and effectiveness of job searching. The post claims that Poozle can help job seekers escape the traditional 'easy apply' black hole and secure more interviews with less effort.

comments.post_body:
Most people are still prompting GPT-5 like it’s GPT-4.

That’s why they’re only getting average answers.

Here's the big shift that happened:

ChatGPT-3.5/4: You could type one vague line and get something decent.
ChatGPT-5: It demands structure and rewards you with smarter, more reliable results.

The 6-Part GPT-5 Prompt Formula by Open AI

➤ Role – Tell it who to be
➤ Task – Define the exact outcome you want
➤ Context – Give background + constraints
➤ Reasoning – Ask it to explain / validate answers
➤ Output Format – Specify tables, bullets, steps, etc.
➤ Stop Conditions – Set when the task is complete


GPT-5 doesn’t just “guess a reply.”
It:
➤ Thinks step-by-step before answering
➤ Uses context much better
➤ Produces cleaner, structured outputs
➤ Saves you from endless back-and-forth edits

4. Example:
Act as a travel guide. 
Find 3 lesser-known hikes within 2 hours of San Francisco. 
Exclude tourist hotspots. 
Cross-check with credible sources. 
Return results in a Markdown table: Hike name | Address | Time | Summary. 
Stop when 3 verified hikes are listed.

If you want average, prompt GPT-5 like GPT-4.
If you want next-level answers, use the 6-part structure.

Check the slide deck below for more detailed explanation.

Check the last slide for the full prompt by Open AI.

Repost this to help others in your network
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the importance of using a structured prompt formula when interacting with GPT-5, as it demands more specificity and rewards with smarter and more reliable results, and the commenter is responding to this post by presumably agreeing or adding to the discussion about optimizing GPT-5 prompts. The 6-part GPT-5 prompt formula is highlighted as a key to achieving next-level answers from the AI model.

comments.comment_body:
Often times, I saw AI is useful is setting up the boiler plate code which is starting from scratch as opposed to fitting into existing code base is still not there to its full potential as we expect AI to be at.

It is good at giving the entire context which I feel can significantly improve the coding time if I were to code on my own as mentioned above reducing time to research.
comments.post_body:
I'm a little late to the party, but I finally put AI to the test on a real coding project.

The project is a support tool for our development team, so low risk if something went wrong. I asked Claude to improve some WPF canvas code by adding an animated antenna to represent signals from an offshore vessel, along with click events.

It took several iterations. Sometimes the code didn’t compile, sometimes I fixed the bugs myself, and sometimes I just made tweaks by hand (which was faster than re-prompting AI). In the end, the antenna looked good. The code was a bit messy, but then again, the original codebase wasn't exactly pretty either, so maybe the AI was just following our conventions. 😅

My takeaways from this first round of AI-assisted coding:

- Great for exploration. It surfaced APIs and language features I hadn’t seen before.

- Not great for polish. Expect to refactor if you care about clean code.

- Less useful when you already know the solution. Typing has never been the bottleneck.

The biggest time saver was discovering parts of the WPF Canvas API I didn’t know. Normally, I’d have learned this more slowly through searches and documentation. Still, I believe it's important to take time to study those new concepts that AI introduces us to. Otherwise we aren't really qualified to evaluate the quality of what AI produces.

For me, AI fit neatly into the early phase of problem-solving: piecing together a solution while expanding my knowledge. Once I understood the problem and knew what "good" looked like, it was easier to take over, clean things up, and bring the code up to company standards. Once the solution became clear, prompting AI further felt less like engineering and more like pulling a slot machine lever.

I know I still have a lot to learn in this new programming paradigm. How does this line up with your experience?
comments.context_summary: The author shares their experience using AI to assist with a coding project, highlighting its benefits for exploration and discovery, but also noting its limitations in producing polished code and its reduced usefulness when the solution is already known. The author is seeking feedback on their experience and how it aligns with others in this new programming paradigm.

𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝘀 𝗘𝗩𝗘𝗥𝗬𝗧𝗛𝗜𝗡𝗚: 𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗖𝗵𝗮𝘁𝗚𝗣𝗧 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗹𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗲𝗺𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀

OpenAI recognizes that people often use ChatGPT when they’re going through mental or emotional distress but chatbot’s responses haven’t always been helpful.

So here’s how they are taking action:

✓ They’re working with mental health experts to help ChatGPT better recognize signs of emotional distress
✓ The chatbot will offer evidence-based resources instead of vague reassurances
✓ For long sessions, it’ll prompt users to take breaks, moving towards a healthier usage

OpenAI is trying to build safer AI as more people form emotional connections with chatbots.
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how OpenAI is working to improve ChatGPT's handling of users' emotional distress by collaborating with mental health experts and providing evidence-based resources. The commenter is responding to this announcement, which aims to build safer AI as users form emotional connections with chatbots.

comments.post_body:
EVERYONE talks about AI Agents but 99.9% don’t know where to start?

Here’s is the ONLY roadmap you need — from GenAI basics to building multi‑agent systems.

🔹 Step 1: Foundations
• GenAI introduction – what it is & why it matters
• Basics of LLMs – architecture & capabilities
• Prompt Engineering – crafting effective inputs

🔹 Step 2: Core Skills
• Intro to API Wrappers – integrate models into apps
• RAG Essentials – boost LLM accuracy w/ your data
• Data handling & processing – clean, transform, store

🔹 Step 3: Agent Fundamentals
• Intro to AI Agents – what makes them “agentic”
• Learn agentic frameworks – LangChain, LlamaIndex, AutoGen
• Build your first simple AI Agent

🔹 Step 4: Going Deeper
• Agentic Memory – short-term vs long-term recall
• Agentic Workflow – orchestrating multi-step tasks
• Evaluation – test, debug, refine agent behavior

🔹 Step 5: Advanced Skills
• Multi‑agent collaboration – agents talking to agents
• Agentic RAG – retrieval‑augmented multi‑agent systems
Now you’re ready to build powerful AI agents for real‑world use 

💡 Remember:
Learning AI Agents isn’t just about coding. It’s about thinking in systems — combining LLMs, tools, memory, and workflows to act autonomously.

NOW you are ready to get to building !

👇 Want a step-by-step tutorial + guide on build AI Agents and multi-agent system from SCRATCH?

Comment “Agents” and I’ll DM you the complete guide.
2) LIKE this post
3) Make sure we are CONNECTED so I can DM you.
4) Repost (if you want to get it faster)

& follow for everything related to AI Agents!

The commenter is likely interested in learning more about AI agents and how to build them.

💡 Stop paying for OpenAI or Anthropic API calls. With DeepSeek R1 and Ollama, you can run powerful reasoning models locally and connect them directly to your n8n workflows completely free.

Here’s how it works:
1️⃣ Download and install Ollama on your computer.
2️⃣ Pull the DeepSeek R1 model (choose from different sizes based on your system).
3️⃣ Run it locally in your terminal and start chatting just like ChatGPT, but without any cost.
4️⃣ Open n8n, switch the chat model to Ollama, and select DeepSeek R1.

Now your n8n AI agents can process tasks, answer questions, and automate workflows all while running 100% on your own machine with zero API fees. Perfect for anyone building serious automation stacks without monthly bills.

I vibe-coded for 72 hours straight, no sleep.
Built an AI monster with 437 agents chained together.
What took 10,000 employees last year? 

Now takes this one system.

It scrapes every single comment on the internet. 
Yeah, all of it.

Across 250+ platforms: TikTok, Insta, YouTube, LinkedIn, X, Facebook, Reddit, Pinterest, MySpace (for the nostalgia scrape), OnlyFans, and more. 

→ analyzes sentiment down to the emoji level
→ predicts virality with 0.02% accuracy
→ generates content that's somehow worse than my drafts
→ books meetings I don't want
→ takes my sales calls (and loses all of them)

I'm pretty sure this thing is sentient now.

comment “behemoth” and I’ll send you the entire n8n flow

comments.comment_body:
Brilliantly articulated, Priyanka. The distinction between automation and true agentic behavior is crucial, especially as more systems begin to exhibit goal-oriented autonomy, not just task execution.

Curious to hear, how do you see agentic AI transforming enterprise cloud architecture and message-driven systems over the next few years
comments.post_body:
🚨 𝐌𝐨𝐬𝐭 𝐩𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐥𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐤 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐲 𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐜 𝐀𝐈. 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐲 𝐝𝐨𝐧'𝐭. Here's my take: https://lnkd.in/d7bRsgFS

I've been watching the AI space explode, and there's a dangerous misconception spreading like wildfire.

Everyone's talking about "Agentic AI" but using it incorrectly:
• ❌ Calling basic chatbots "agentic"
• ❌ Thinking any automated system is "agentic"
• ❌ Missing what makes AI truly autonomous

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵: True agentic AI doesn't just follow commands - it makes independent decisions, adapts strategies, and pursues goals autonomously.

The implications for business, careers, and innovation are massive. But only if you understand what agentic AI actually IS.

🎥 I break down the real definition and show you what to look for: https://lnkd.in/d7bRsgFS

This isn't just semantics - understanding true agentic AI will determine who thrives in the next wave of innovation.

What's your experience with AI systems that claim to be "agentic"? 👇

♻️ If you found this useful: I regularly share Cloud & AI insights (through my newsletter subscribe here https://lnkd.in/dRifnnex) hit follow (Priyanka Vergadia) and feel free to share it so others can learn too!

#AgenticAI #ArtificialIntelligence #AIInnovation #MachineLearning #TechLeadership
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the misconception of what constitutes 'Agentic AI' and how most people misunderstand its true meaning, which refers to AI that makes independent decisions and adapts strategies autonomously. The author is responding to the general misconception and sharing their take on the correct definition of Agentic AI.

comments.post_body:
Top 10 Database Scaling Techniques You Should Know:

1. 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐱𝐢𝐧𝐠: Create indexes on frequently queried columns to speed up data retrieval.

2. 𝐕𝐞𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐥 𝐒𝐜𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐠: Upgrade your database server by adding more CPU, RAM, or storage to handle increased load.

3. 𝐂𝐚𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠: Store frequently accessed data in-memory (e.g., Redis) to reduce database load and improve response time.

4. 𝐒𝐡𝐚𝐫𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠: Distribute data across multiple servers by splitting the database into smaller, independent shards, allowing for horizontal scaling and improved performance.

5. 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧: Create multiple copies (replicas) of the database across different servers, enabling read queries to be distributed across replicas and improving availability.

6. 𝐐𝐮𝐞𝐫𝐲 𝐎𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧: Fine-tune SQL queries, eliminate expensive operations, and leverage indexes effectively to improve execution speed and reduce database load.

7. 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐧𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐏𝐨𝐨𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐠: Reduce the overhead of opening/closing database connections by reusing existing ones, improving performance under heavy traffic.

8. 𝐕𝐞𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐥 𝐏𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠: Split large tables into smaller, more manageable parts (partitions), each containing a subset of the columns from the original table.

9. 𝐃𝐞𝐧𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧: Store data in a redundant but structured format to minimize complex joins and speed up read-heavy workloads.

10. 𝐌𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐳𝐞𝐝 𝐕𝐢𝐞𝐰𝐬: Pre-compute and store results of complex queries as separate tables to avoid expensive recalculation, reducing database load and improving response times.

♻️ Repost to help others in your network

👉 Get a FREE System Design Interview Handbook in your inbox by subscribing to my newsletter: blog.algomaster.io
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the top 10 database scaling techniques, including indexing, vertical scaling, caching, and more, to improve database performance and efficiency. The commenter is responding to the original post, which provides a comprehensive list of techniques for optimizing database scalability.

comments.post_body:
7 out of 10 businesses are missing the AI automation opportunity.
(𝗜 𝘀𝗲𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝗺𝘆 𝘀𝗮𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘀.)

So many are stuck in manual processes.
Wondering why competitors are suddenly 10x faster.

But this 5-step AI automation process will help you out.

Here's what business leaders should be implementing: 

STEP 1: IDENTIFY AI USE CASES
↳ Content Creation with Jasper, Copy(.)ai
↳ Data Analysis with Julius AI, Code Interpreter  
↳ Customer Support with Retell AI, Vectorshift AI

STEP 2: SELECT AI MODEL/TOOL
↳ ChatGPT for conversations & images
↳ Claude for analysis & coding
↳ n8n for workflow automation

STEP 3: DESIGN AGENT WORKFLOW
↳ Map the process with clear inputs/outputs
↳ Define decision logic
↳ Set up error handling

STEP 4: CONNECT APIs & DATA
↳ Zapier for app connections
↳ Make for workflow automation
↳ Langchain for AI frameworks

STEP 5: DEPLOY & MONITOR
↳ Track performance metrics
↳ Optimize based on results
↳ Scale what works

KEY TECH SKILLS YOU NEED:
- API Integration & Webhooks
- Prompt Engineering (ChatGPT, Claude)
- No-Code Platforms (Lovable, n8n)
- Vector Databases
- AI Agent Architecture

TOP CREATORS TO STAY UP-TO-DATE:
1. Edgar Perez
2. Bernard Marr
3. Allie K. Miller
4. Andrew Ng
5. Henry Ajder
6. Aishwarya Srinivasan
7. Brij kishore Pandey
8. Ethan Mollick

Success isn't in using every AI tool.
It's in building the RIGHT automations for YOUR business.

Most companies dabble with ChatGPT while missing
the opportunity to automate entire workflows.

This 5-step process cuts through the hype.
It gives you a clear path to 10x productivity.
And the competitive edge to dominate your niche.

Over to you: Which process are you automating first?
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how 7 out of 10 businesses are missing the AI automation opportunity and provides a 5-step process to help them implement AI automation, from identifying use cases to deploying and monitoring AI models. The commenter is responding to this post, which aims to help businesses cut through the hype and achieve 10x productivity through AI automation.

comments.comment_body: Thanks for sharing your inspiring journey that can inspire many others 🙂. To me, rejection can’t let you down, it is our efforts/believes that will lead to success/failure.
comments.post_body:
I’m the person who got rejected by Northeastern University but got selected by Duke University & Harvard Business School.  

I’m the person who got rejected by a startup in India right before moving, and then got an offer letter from Visa in U.S.

I’m the person whose book got rejected by a major publishing house in India just because I wasn’t a famous poet, but who published her own book despite that.

There’s one story of rejection that my father has told me multiple times whenever a dream of mine has remained unfulfilled — the story of Shri APJ Abdul Kalam. 

He was rejected from Indian Air Force and couldn’t turn his dream of becoming an IAF pilot into reality. 

He ended up becoming the President of India. 

“Rejections are redirections” is a statement that holds NO value for me.

For me, rejections mean NOTHING. 

So, the next time you get rejected, remember this : 

Someone can reject your CV for a 100 reasons if they don’t have the lens to read you; but someone else who’s equipped to see your value will see it right the first time. 

Be that someone who knows their own value. 

And, keep building yourself. Keep working on yourself. 

Because rejections … they mean nothing. :)

P.S.  I’m Shreya Mishra Reddy and I write about my experiences and learnings. Follow along.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about overcoming rejection, sharing their own experiences of being rejected by prestigious institutions and companies, but ultimately achieving success, and encouraging others to remember their own value and keep working on themselves despite rejections. The post emphasizes that rejections do not define one's worth and can often lead to new opportunities.

comments.post_body:
🔥 𝗥𝗔𝗚 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿’𝘀 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸 — 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗡𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗞𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝗕𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 🔍

Building with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is more than just picking an LLM — it’s about designing a modular, scalable, and future-ready tech stack.

⚙️ Here's a quick breakdown of the modern RAG stack (credit: Kalyan KS):

🔹 LLMs (Open vs Closed)
 → Open: LLaMA 3, Phi-4, Mistral (⚙️ control, customization)
 → Closed: OpenAI, Claude, Gemini (🚀 performance, simplicity)
 🎯 Tradeoff: Flexibility vs Convenience

🔹 Frameworks
 → LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack, txtai — the glue for chaining, memory, routing, & tool-use logic.

🔹 Vector Databases
 → Chroma, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus
 ⚡ Powering fast, relevant, scalable retrieval.

🔹 Data Extraction (Web + Docs)
 → Crawl4AI, FireCrawl, LlamaParse, Docling
 📥 Raw data = richer context = better answers.

🔹 Open LLM Access Platforms
 → Hugging Face, Ollama, Groq, Together AI
 🔧 Simplifying infra & accelerating experimentation.

🔹 Text Embeddings
 → Open: Nomic, SBERT, BGE
 → Proprietary: OpenAI, Google, Cohere
 📐 Retrieval quality starts here.

🔹 Evaluation
 → Ragas, Trulens, Giskard
 🔍 Measure hallucination, grounding, relevance, and model behavior.

🧠 𝗧𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆:
 RAG ≠ integration problem. RAG = design problem.

 Every layer affects latency, explainability, quality, and cost.

💬 What does your #RAG stack look like today?

#GenAI #RAGStack #AIInfrastructure #LangChain #LlamaIndex #LLMs #OpenSourceAI #VectorSearch #AIFrameworks #MachineLearning #AIStack #PromptEngineering #AIObservability #Embeddings #RAG #MLOps #AIEngineering #ArtificialIntelligence
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the importance of designing a modular and scalable tech stack for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) development, highlighting key components such as LLMs, frameworks, and vector databases. The commenter is responding to the post's breakdown of the modern RAG stack and the tradeoffs involved in building with RAG.

comments.post_body:
Recently, I thought of using AI to help with some production work. The task was fairly simple — I needed to update an existing CDK package to add a new S3 stack.
I didn’t spend much time diving into the codebase; instead, I asked the AI to analyze the repo and implement the required changes. Initially, it did a great job — it generated code that looked correct at first glance. However, when I tried to build it, I faced some challenges. With a few prompt iterations and by passing the error logs, the AI was eventually able to fix the bugs, and the build succeeded.

Two days later, I realized that the new S3 stack was missing permissions, so I had to add them manually. While reviewing the existing code, I discovered something surprising: the entire CDK codebase was structured using the Factory Pattern. Ideally, the correct way to add the new stack was to create a new factory. Instead, the AI had created the stack differently, ignoring the established pattern.
Now, I have to raise a new change request — removing the AI-generated code and rewriting it properly using the factory approach. 😅

My two cents:
Using AI to generate code can be powerful and time-saving, but if you don’t understand the underlying code architecture, the solution may not align with your team’s standards or be scalable. Also, when things break, debugging AI-generated code can take longer than fixing bugs in code you wrote yourself.
comments.context_summary: The author shares their experience of using AI to update a CDK package, which initially seemed successful but ultimately required manual corrections due to the AI's lack of understanding of the codebase's architecture. The author concludes that while AI can be powerful, it's crucial to understand the underlying code architecture to ensure scalability and maintainability.
